article_title: Confronting trade-offs and interactive effects in the choice of policy focus: Specialized versus comprehensive private governance

It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Confronting trade-offs and interactive effects in the choice of policy focus: Specialized versus comprehensive private governance Graeme Auld School of Public Policy and Administration, Carleton University, Ottawa, ON, Canada Abstract In setting standards for responsible business practices, certiﬁcation programs create issue boundaries delineated by the focus of their standards. These issue boundaries may impede action on certain causes of problems (i.e. problem interactive effects) or lead to policy actions that affect other governance initiatives (i.e. policy interactive effects).When these interactions are extensive, programs confront trade-offs: develop as a comprehensive program (i.e. have a broad policy focus) and take on higher internal administrative costs, or develop as a specialized program (i.e. have a narrow policy focus) and undertake to develop mechanisms to facilitate across-program coordination. This paper explores these trade-offs. It examines the origins of the different policy foci of coffee, forest, and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation programs, and identiﬁes ﬁve strategies that programs are currently using to manage policy and/or problem interactive effects. Then, informed by research in public administration and international relations, it details additional approaches for improving issue-boundary management. Keywords: certiﬁcation, environmental governance, institutional design, interactive effects, private governance. 1. Introduction Certiﬁcation programs – formed and run by non-state actors – are becoming potential sources of global regulation for many social and environmental problems. Issuing standards for respon- sible practices in various economic sectors, these programs attempt to generate market beneﬁts for those operators that voluntarily participate and pass an independent inspection audit (cf. Bartley 2007b). By some accounts, certiﬁcation represents a geographically unrestricted means of governance matched to the supply-chain-centered organization of global markets (Bernstein & Cashore 2007; Tollefson et al. 2008). Yet they are not free of limits. Each program chooses a scope of issues to address and a domain of actors to regulate. Thus, while they may avoid interstate cooperation problems, they simultaneously engender issue boundaries. A division of labor that permits focusing on one of many environmental or social problems may enhance effectiveness. Equally, however, issue boundaries at the margin or intersection of certiﬁcation programs may: impede effective attention to certain causes of problems (i.e. problem interactive effects); or lead to policy actions by one program that affect other programs, and vice versa (i.e. policy interactive effects). Hence, if problem and policy interactions across Correspondence: Graeme Auld, School of Public Policy and Administration, Carleton University, 5206 River Building, 1125 Colonel By Drive, Ottawa, ON, Canada K1S5B6. Managing these issues may then require working with other programs, creating coordination costs. A comprehensive program focusing on many issues, by contrast, will better handle problem interactions and avoid policy interactive effects. Yet it will face higher internal admin- istrative costs. This paper examines these trade-offs by asking three questions, two positive and one nor- mative. Why do certiﬁcation programs adopt a given policy focus? What strategies are programs using to overcome the challenges their issue boundaries present? And ﬁnally, what additional strategies could be used to help improve the management of these boundaries? The analysis traces the origins of programs’ choices of policy foci to the different institu- tional constraints and characters, capabilities, and preferences of program founders. It then identiﬁes ﬁve strategies that programs are currently using to manage policy and/or problem interactive effects, moving from unilateral strategies to those that involve considerable across- program coordination. The situation confronting private governance parallels similar challenges for international organizations and public bureaucracies. Drawing particularly on Oberthür’s (2009) work on managing the interplay among international institutions, the paper identiﬁes direct and indirect mechanisms for autonomous interplay management wherein governments, civil society organizations, businesses, and other actors may assist in managing issue-boundary challenges. Finally, the analysis considers how the early governance designs of certiﬁcation programs may be subject to path dependence and ﬁnds that certiﬁcation programs are collec- tively managing issue boundaries in a way that appears to mitigate potential path dependence on the narrow concern of policy focus. A key contribution of the analysis is to distinguish interactions between programs with similar policy foci from interactions between programs with dissimilar policy foci (cf. Eberlein et al. 2014). Previous research has focused considerable attention on the former – competitive interactions among programs focused on a similar policy issue (e.g. Overdevest 2010) – where coordination may lead a program with higher standards to water them down (Humphreys 2006; Raynolds et al. 2007). A focus on interactions between programs with different policy foci highlights another side of program-to-program relations, one where coordination, rather than competition, may prove beneﬁcial from a problem-oriented perspective. The empirics for the study are coffee, forest, and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation programs, some of which have existed for upwards of 20 years. The analysis is based on research interviews with program developers and managers from the three sectors, as well as an examination of primary and secondary documents. The importance of coordination emerged from a broader study of the institutional evolution of certiﬁcation programs and the potential path-dependent effects of early design choices (see Auld 2009). Building from this study, the paper presents an inductive analysis of how programs are confronting the problem of coordination and, informed by existing theories, how coordination might be improved. By examining the policy focus of programs – a variable thus far given limited attention – the analysis to follow contributes to work assessing the independent and intersecting effects of certiﬁcation initiatives (e.g. Gulbrandsen 2009; Marx & Cuypers 2010). The analysis proceeds in three parts. First, it reviews the emergence of private governance and certiﬁcation initiatives, explains the relevance of issue boundaries as a factor shaping the effects of certiﬁcation programs, and describes the trade-off between specialization and com- prehensiveness that programs face when choosing a policy focus. Second, it examines two Trade-offs and interactive effects G. Turning to its ﬁnal question, the paper offers recommendations on how to manage future program interactions. 2. The problem-oriented effects of policy focus within private governance The realm of private governance has broadened immensely in recent decades, with numerous codes of conduct, self-regulatory programs, and private–private partnerships forming to govern the environmental and ethical impacts of business activities (Vogel 2008; Utting et al. 2012). Although linked to the deregulation and privatization agenda of neoliberalism seen to undercut the state, Levi-Faur (2005) notes that this agenda has, in fact, spurred reregulation, creating what he terms “regulatory capitalism.” Rather than fewer rules, dense networks of interlocking regu- lation – public and private – are now in play. These form the justiﬁcation for increased scholarly attention to how policy mixes (Gunningham & Grabosky 1998), activity clusters (Hoffmann 2011), and regime complexes (Alter & Meunier 2009) operate to address policy problems at multiple scales. In the ﬁeld of private initiatives, non-state certiﬁcation programs (hereafter certiﬁcation programs) have become a focal point of interest (e.g. Bartley 2007b; Bernstein & Cashore 2007). These initiatives comprise four key features. First, they have logos or labels that can be used on products sold to end-consumers. Second, they have some form of inspection and monitoring, which may have begun as an internal process, but over time typically becomes a third-party veriﬁcation process. Third, they have governance structures and procedures for overseeing operations, including rules for membership, decisionmaking, setting and revising standards, accrediting auditors, and resolving disputes. Finally, and most relevant to this paper, the pro- grams have standards regulating social and/or environmental impacts of production processes, as well as tracking requirements for following products through to the end-consumer. The policy focus of these certiﬁcation standards has two components: program scope, which is deﬁned as the substantive policy problems a program regulates; and program domain, deﬁned as the group of economic actors in a supply chain that the program’s rules target.1 Certiﬁcation programs have galvanized interest in part for the contribution they might make to the formation of a nascent global public domain (Ruggie 2004; Bernstein & Cashore 2007). A world of autonomous, sovereign states poses challenges for environmental and social problems. Because these problems can cross national boundaries or feed off inter-state competition for capital investments and job creation, states need to cooperate to address them (Young 1999). Certiﬁcation programs are seen as potentially powerful tools to address such global problems, less encumbered by the geographic restrictions of the state system (Cashore 2002). However, the policy focus chosen by certiﬁcation programs creates new boundaries – those deﬁned by the respective policy foci of different certiﬁcation programs – and these new boundaries have possible problem-oriented effects. 2.1. The problem-oriented effects of issue boundaries Four broad variables have received attention by research on the adoption of and compliance with certiﬁcation rules. First, market demand is seen as critical. Because certiﬁcation programs are voluntary, unless there is some economic reason for an operator to participate, the ability of certiﬁcation to do more than differentiate operators already practicing responsibly is considered Trade-offs and interactive effects G. Auld © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 128 Page 4: a challenge (Cashore et al. 2007; Gulbrandsen 2010). Explanatory work has examined: the role of NGO targeting campaigns in securing commitments from large corporate buyers to leverage demands for certiﬁcation up global supply chains (Sasser et al. 2006); the role of US foundations in organizing and funding these NGO campaigns (Bartley 2007a); and the possible role of demand from end consumers (e.g. Loureiro et al. 2003). Second, considerable attention is given to the stringency of the certiﬁcation rules (McDermott et al. 2008; Fransen 2011). The premise here is that stringent rules have positive effects, and that these are going to be costly for operators to meet, which means control measures are needed to ensure compliance and effectiveness. Hence, stringency can broadly be seen to encompass the character of certiﬁcation rules and the procedures in place to ensure operator compliance. Explanatory work explores why some programs adopt stringent rules; several studies note that those programs controlled or originated by business have less stringent requirements (Cashore et al. 2004; Gulbrandsen 2010; Fransen 2011). Other studies examine why the mechanism of third party certiﬁcation has emerged in different sectors (e.g. Bartley 2007b). Third, scholars have examined supply-side factors, particularly the uneven capabilities of operators seeking certiﬁcation. Here the idea is that not all operators will have equal abilities to meet voluntary standards. Research has shown a skew in certiﬁcation participation toward larger, vertically integrated operators that face higher – and enforced – government standards (Cashore et al. 2006). Links to international networks of NGOs, funders, and foundations also matter, as these connections can help operators overcome barriers to entry, including audit costs and technical know-how (Pattberg 2006). Additionally, the ﬁt of the production practices in a region with certiﬁcation requirements affects compliance costs. For instance, a history of shade-grown coffee as compared to sun-coffee (Mas & Dietsch 2004) or a history of organized cooperatives (Nigh 1997) each make it easier for coffee growers to participate in bird-friendly and fair trade certiﬁcation, respectively. Finally, regulatory competition among certiﬁcation initiatives has been examined to deter- mine whether this promotes a race to the top, middle, or bottom. Perspectives on the effects of competition vary. Overdevest (2010) shows how competitive benchmarking has led to an increase in the standards of producer-backed programs in the forest sector. Others point to downward pressure on standards as a consequence of competition (e.g. Raynolds et al. 2007; Bitzer et al. 2008). Current work, however, pays little attention to the problem-oriented implications of the boundaries created by the policy focus of certiﬁcation initiatives (for exceptions, see Visseren-Hamakers & Glasbergen 2007; Bitzer et al. 2008). This paper argues that this variable deserves more attention. It is particularly important because environmental and social problems are not only transboundary; they are also complex, multifaceted, and interrelated with social and ecological systems, which are often coupled in ways difﬁcult for policy to capture. 2.2. The specialization–comprehensiveness trade-offs One way to understand the implications of a policy focus is by considering the trade-offs between specialization and comprehensiveness. Specialization has costs and beneﬁts. Optimizing across a range of policy objectives can lead to unanticipated trade-offs and compromises that may systematically harm a particular aim. Specialization can also help agencies or departments build needed expertise to competently address speciﬁc issues. However, when coordination to address a larger problem arises, the issue boundaries of specialized programs become sites for conﬂict (Grumbine 1991; Kavanagh & Richards 2001; Christensen & Lægreid 2007). Auld © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 129 Page 5: Research in public administration reveals the policymaking challenge presented by formal boundaries among bureaucratic agencies and departments. It ﬁnds that interactions and infor- mation ﬂows within agencies or departments are more likely to occur than ﬂows across their boundaries (Egeberg 1999). Indeed, the issue of coordination across boundaries is a broad challenge not limited to countries’ territorial or geographic borders or to inter-departmental bickering. Egeberg (1994), drawing on the work of Hammond (1986) and Gulick (1937), argues that no hierarchy is neutral. Rather, the organizing principle for a set of interacting hierarchies, be it geography (e.g. countries in the international system), sector (e.g. government departments focused on sectors such as energy or ﬁsheries), or function (e.g. a department of justice or regulatory agencies), affects the types of conﬂicts that will be dealt with by the governance system as a whole (Egeberg 2003). For any problem-oriented institution, therefore, questions of ﬁt become immediately relevant and will hinge notably on how a given problem is identiﬁed, understood, and framed (Pal 2001; Young 2002, pp. 20–22, 113). These insights about the challenges created by boundaries highlight the importance of two types of interactive effects. First, problem interactive effects exist where two problems are inter- related. Forest degradation is often driven by agricultural activities; hence, to address forest issues, agricultural activities require attention (Curran et al. 2004). Dealing with trafﬁc conges- tion is interlinked with environmental and human health concerns, as city, road, and transit design affects whether citizens will walk, ride, or bus to work and the extent to which ground level air pollution becomes a health problem. Climate change, as a policy problem, is replete with interactions, as changes in the frequency and intensity of storms, temperature variability, and ocean acidity, for instance, have implications for agriculture production, ﬁsheries, city infra- structure, and the insurance industry. Problem interactions capture couplings among social, economic, and political processes that make disentangling problems into independent parts difﬁcult, if not impossible. Second, policy interactive effects exist where a policy response to a given problem affects the policy responses to other problems.2 These can be positive or negative. Many scholars examine interactions between national policies – for instance, the interactions of different countries’ Internet regulations (Goldsmith 2000) – but this is also relevant across policy issue areas – for instance, increased security measures to address the threat of terrorism and how these affect immigration policy (Pump 2011) or a pollution tax that facilitates the lowering of distortionary taxes but maintains government revenues through a revenue recycling effect (Goulder et al. 1997). The rules of the World Trade Organization (WTO), particularly those affecting policies that national governments set on process-related concerns, are an example of a policy inter- active effect that limits domestic policy options on environmental and social issues (Kysar 2004); WTO requirements against discriminating in favor of domestic goods or the goods of certain countries over others (i.e. the most-favored nation treatment), can also limit the fea- sibility of using trade sanctions to enforce multilateral environmental agreements (Oberthür 2009). Given these effects, the specialization–comprehensiveness trade-off creates a dilemma for certiﬁcation decisionmakers. On the one hand, a program that chooses to specialize and focus on a narrow problem will face reduced administrative costs, but may neglect important problem interactive effects, and potentially even create policy interactive effects for other governance initiatives. To manage these problems, therefore, specialized programs will have to coordinate their activities with other specialized programs, or they will need to develop duplicative struc- tures to mitigate the extra-sectoral causes of the problems occurring for their specialized policy focus. On the other hand, a program taking a comprehensive approach will be better able to Trade-offs and interactive effects G. Auld © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 130 Page 6: manage complex, across-problem interactions and to address potential policy interactive effects. At the same time, its administrative costs will rise.3 Figure 1 captures this trade-off, illustrating how the degree of problem and/or policy inter- active effects inﬂuence the problem ﬁt of specialized and comprehensive programs. This degree of ﬁt has implications for the internal administrative costs and across-program coordination costs we can expect to observe. Internal administrative costs are deﬁned as the “costs of orga- nizing resources” within a certiﬁcation program to get things done; across-program coordina- tion costs are deﬁned as “the anticipated organizational complexity of decomposing tasks among partners along with ongoing coordination activities to be completed jointly or indi- vidually across organizational boundaries and the related extent of communication and deci- sions that would be necessary” (Gulati & Singh 1998, p. 782). In quadrant A, low levels of problem and policy interactive effects mean specialized programs are closely ﬁt with the char- acter of the problem and, hence, will face few internal administrative costs and low across- program coordination costs. By contrast, comprehensive programs in quadrant B are a poorer ﬁt given the limited problem and policy interactive effects. Quadrant C captures cases with high problem and/or policy interactive effects. For this case, comprehensive programs will face high internal administrative costs, but they will ﬁt well the character of the problem. Quadrant D, by comparison, represents the case where specialized programs will face high across- program coordination costs in order to address the high problem and/or policy interactive effects. The relationships captured in Figure 1 imply that specialized programs are appropriate in instances where the level of potential problem and/or policy interactive effects is low and when the costs of coordinating across programs are lower than the administrative costs involved in establishing and running a comprehensive program. The following discussion examines the institutional constraints and actor preferences that shaped these domain and scope speciﬁcations. 3.1.1. Institutional constraints There were several ways in which the choices of scope and domain were linked to existing institutional structures. First, these initiatives were affected by the existing deﬁnitions of sectors, which are shaped by government polices, programs, and departments, as well as the holdings of companies, technologies for extraction, cultivation, production and transportation, professional associations, and educational programs. For instance, the GAA was launched during a meeting of the World Aquaculture Society in 1997, which is a society comprising scientists and repre- sentatives of the aquaculture industry (Christensen 1997). Similarly, many universities have separate agriculture and forestry departments, which reinforce the divisions between these ﬁelds of practice, rather than highlighting overlaps and interactions. Second, existing standards also served as templates for what should constitute the appro- priate policy focus of certiﬁcation standards. With the MSC, the drafters of the principles and criteria drew from existing and developing standards including the Food and Agriculture Organization’s (FAO) Code of Conduct for Responsible Fisheries, the UN Fish Stocks Agree- ment, and the Principles for the Conservation of Wild Living Resources (Sutton 1996; OECD 2005). Those involved in drafting the Forest Stewardship Council (FSC) principles and criteria used the International Tropical Timber Organization’s (1992) Guidelines for the Sus- tainable Management of Natural Tropical Forests as a reference point for their work (Synnott 2005). FLO built its standard from long-standing ideas of fair trade used by alternative trade organizations. Third, the choices of program founders were also shaped by what government policies and programs and other initiatives were already addressing. In this way, certiﬁcation emerged to ﬁll legal or policy gaps. As an example, with forestry, two problems were particularly salient in the late 1980s. The ﬁrst concerned the release of toxic chemicals as a by-product of pulp and paper production processes (Harrison 2002). The second involved the degradation and loss of intact forests – commonly referred to as old growth – around the world (Humphreys 1996). Both issues sparked international campaigns calling for product boycotts (Stanbury 2000), and yet when it came to the development of forest certiﬁcation, the focus narrowed to forest management issues, not the life cycle of a given wood product or other concerns, such as worker safety in manufac- turing facilities. As one FSC founder explained: “we felt there was a real gap on the forest management side and there were potentially other tools to deal with workers’ rights, to deal with environmental impacts of processing, whether those were eco-labels or some of the ISO standards.”4 Trade-offs and interactive effects G. The character, capabilities, and preferences of actors Within the context of the above constraints, the preferences of actors involved in forming certiﬁcation initiatives also mattered for the policy focus of different initiatives. With the MSC, Unilever and the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF) were key. That Unilever chose to work with WWF underscores the strength of the actor-centered perspective. Prior to working with the WWF, Unilever had been under pressure from Greenpeace, which began an oceans campaign in 1996. The group’s campaign against industrial ﬁshing in the North Sea led both Unilever and Sainsbury’s to commit to ending their use of ﬁsh oils and meals from North Sea ﬁsheries (Brown 1996). Rather than work with Greenpeace on a ﬁsheries sustainability initiative, however, Unilever was advised that WWF would be a more amicable partner (Hamprecht 2005). Taking this path, the two organizations worked to create a certiﬁcation program modeled after the FSC. Each partner came with different preferences and capabilities. The WWF had already been involved in founding the FSC, and saw a similar program for seafood as a key strategic component of its “endangered seas campaign” (Flanders 1998). Unilever, for its part, had been receiving scientiﬁc advice on the sustainability of ﬁsh stocks in the North Sea, which were a concern for its long-term supply (Hamprecht 2005, p. 100). Though by no means dictating the policy focus that the MSC took, the preferences of these two organizations did affect the subsequent process for drafting the MSC standards. Fair trade programs that developed in various European countries in the late 1980s and early 1990s, and eventually cooperated to produce FLO, also illustrate the effects of founders’ prefer- ences. Capabilities have been another consideration of certiﬁcation founders. There were at least two ways in which this shaped program choices. The ﬁrst concerned expertise. Although good reasons might have existed for developing a standard with a scope broad enough to encompass interactive effects, founders may have lacked the capabilities to undertake such a project. As one FSC founder explained: “[we] thought it was probably overly ambitious to try to cover, you know, the full life cycle of wood from forests to recycling to bin, or whatever it might be, and that there were other players that were already covering some of that territory, so we didn’t think it was totally necessary.”5 Similarly, certain programs were very aware of the specialization– comprehensiveness trade-off and chose to err on the side of specialization, partly as a conse- quence of considering capabilities. During the meetings held to draft the MSC principles and criteria, ﬁve principles were initially under consideration, one of which dealt with social issues. However, this principle was eventually dropped, in part because there was a perception that the MSC could not solve all marine ﬁsh issues, and that dealing with ﬁsh stocks and biology alone was important and complicated enough.6 This choice to remain specialized also shaped the MSC’s ongoing deliberations about whether or not it should address aquaculture. It ultimately decided only to work on capture ﬁsheries (Bedrosian 2008). 3.2. Balancing and managing the specialization–comprehensiveness trade-offs A major concern about creating specialized certiﬁcation programs lies in their potential inability to adapt over time. If programs are highly ﬂexible – or there is little inertia associated with their Trade-offs and interactive effects G. Auld © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 134 Page 10: initial decisions – they should easily be able to develop coordination strategies to address any problem and/or policy interactive effects arising from their initial policy focus. Coffee, forest, and ﬁsheries certiﬁcation programs have adopted several strategies partly intent on addressing these challenges. Five strategies that stand out are presented below in order of increasing levels of cross-program coordination (Table 2). 3.2.1. Altering program domain or scope A ﬁrst strategy involves changing program requirements, either by modifying the domain of eligible participants or by changing the scope of standards, such that an individual program can deal with some problem and/or policy interactive effects. Two notable illustrations of this strategy are the choices of IFOAM and FLO to extend the scope of their respective standards. IFOAM added requirements for social performance and FLO added basic environmental stan- dards. Each change was partly in reaction to the growing awareness of the interlinkages among social and environmental issues in production practices (Raynolds et al. 2007; Courville 2008).7 In a different manner, the Smithsonian Migratory Bird Center (SMBC) has also tackled a problem interactive effect. It requires that any coffee farmer that wants to be certiﬁed “bird friendly” must also pass an inspection against the US Department of Agriculture organic stan- dard (SMBC 2002). This requirement avoids the program having to develop its own standards to address the use of pesticides or other farm practices, which may also affect the quality of bird habitat on coffee farms. In this respect, each program has taken individual actions to address problem interactive effects. The FSC, on the other hand, provides an example of unilateral action to address a policy interactive effect. When the program launched, it had a policy to only allow products with 100 percent FSC content to carry its label. By adopting this policy, the FSC lacked a means to accommodate recycled ﬁber when promoting responsible paper products.
