Indeed, were they intent on using the FSC label, it might mean they would use harvested ﬁber over recycled ﬁber, a negative interactive effect of this FSC content policy. Changes to the content policy have allowed products with mixed content to carry the label, and in 2004, the FSC began permitting products made with 100 percent post-consumer recycled ﬁber to carry its label (Auld 2006). These changes, while mitigating the negative policy interac- tive effects, have also increased FSC’s administrative complexity. The new content policy also created new interactive effects. Certiﬁers had to ensure that non-FSC content in labeled products did not contain wood from controversial sources, such as timber coming from illegal logging, which would have been automatically excluded under the earlier 100 percent content rule. 3.2.2. Extending to new products A second strategy – a modular approach – involves extending the principles of the program by developing standards for new product groupings. Here the scope of a program’s standards may remain largely the same, but the program extends its domain by making a new group of operators eligible. IFOAM has used this approach most extensively, and it is usually a two-step process. First, one of the many individual certiﬁers accredited to assess organic practices develops a new organic standard for a new sector, often responding to interest from producers seeking coverage by an organic label. Soil Association, Naturland, and Bio-Gro, for instance, did this with aquaculture (Tacon & Brister 2002). Second, once these standards are in place and certiﬁcations are underway, IFOAM typically will develop a general standard for the sector to guide future certiﬁcations. For instance, the Soil Association began drafting aquaculture standards in 1989, and IFOAM adopted a basic standard for organic aquaculture in 2005 (Auld 2009). Organic coffee certiﬁcations were occurring even before IFOAM was established; it then took until 1995 for IFOAM to issue a basic coffee standard (Linton 2005). FLO, by comparison, began by relying on its national labeling initiatives – of which there are currently 19 – to do the initial work developing new product standards. This preliminary work then underwent an approval and assessment process administered by FLO’s standards committee.8 Other programs, including the FSC, GAA, Utz (a program that began by certifying coffee farmers, see Table 1), and the Rainforest Alliance, have also adopted this modular approach. For instance, the FSC began following this approach with forest carbon accounting, considering the appropriateness of a “modular extension.”9 A September 2009 meeting of the Forest Carbon Working Group discussed a strategy for moving this forward. This included a review of the FSC system that would likely uncover needed “adjustments and amendments of the FSC compo- nents” to make the program appropriate for carbon accounting (FSC 2009). Across the programs, this strategy has been used to address problem and policy interactive effects. In the forestry case, one perceived threat for certiﬁcation was the effect forest carbon projects might have on forest ecosystems as a consequence of only considering the carbon- storing potential of the forests, while neglecting their other environmental and social beneﬁts. By working with carbon-offset standards, the expectation is this negative policy interactive effect can be pre-empted. There are also problem interactions between climate change and forests (e.g. increased incidence of beetle outbreaks or changing inter-species competition as a result of an altered temperature regime or the CO2-fertilization effect [Lewis 2006]). This makes the man- agement of forests entwined with climate change mitigation and adaptation. With organics and fair trade, policy interactions can occur at the production or retail end. At the retail end, companies that are concerned about their overall social or environmental reputation can ﬁnd a patchwork of organic or fair trade product offerings problematic, as labeling some products may Trade-offs and interactive effects G. Auld © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 136 Page 12: have the negative effect of implying to consumers that other products are less responsibly produced. 3.2.3. Bilateral coordination A third strategy involves bilateral coordination between certiﬁcation programs to address problem and/or policy interactive effects. Unlike the previous two strategies, here programs begin to build cross-program coordination mechanisms to facilitate ongoing work on common challenges, such as establishing joint decisionmaking protocols and operational procedures. These interactions may build from previous informal interactions. For instance, one FLO ofﬁcial explained that they had, for a long time, attended BioFach – an organic trade fair – where they would interact with IFOAM. The two programs also held joint workshops.10 Bilateral coordination has become more formal as well. For instance, the FSC and FLO began work in 2009 on a fair trade standard for forest management. Participants in the program must already be FSC certiﬁed, and then FLO will develop a standard for timber that adds fair trade considerations not covered by the FSC, including a modiﬁed version of the price- premium requirements (FLO 2010). Through a pilot project, a community forest in Honduras and groups of small and low intensity producers in both Chile and Bolivia have received dual certiﬁcation. Furniture procured from these forests reached German stores early in 2012 (FSC 2013). A related form of coordination involves programs combining their services. For instance, the Soil Association worked with the MSC on sourcing MSC ﬁshmeal as a way to ensure the environmental quality of feed used in organic aquaculture operations (Soil Association 2006). Here the two programs sought to avoid a potential negative policy interactive effect: growing demand for organic aquaculture might drive demand for unsustainably managed ocean-capture ﬁsheries. The FSC’s Forest Carbon Working Group also recognized the need for this kind of coordination (FSC 2009). In September 2012, following the release of a strategy paper on climate change engagement (FSC 2011), the FSC announced a memorandum of understanding with the Gold Standard (a certiﬁcation program for voluntary carbon offset projects) (Gold Standard 2013) to assess an alignment of the two programs to provide certiﬁcation for forest carbon projects (FSC 2012). 3.2.4. Multilateral coordination Multiple programs can also coordinate to address problem and policy interactive effects. The mechanisms to make this possible become more complex as the number of programs increases. The previous three strategies imposed higher within-program administrative costs. Multilateral coordination creates new institutions to undertake these functions, imposing larger across- program coordination costs associated with joint decisionmaking and operations. The best example of this strategy that currently exists is the International Social and Envi- ronmental Accreditation and Labeling (ISEAL) Alliance. Established in 2002, the Alliance brings together certiﬁcation initiatives to advance their common interests, particularly around the methods for setting and evaluating social and environmental standards. By 2011, ISEAL had a budget of 1.3 million euros; a large proportion of these funds are dedicated to its coordination activities (ISEAL Alliance 2012b). ISEAL focuses more on policy than problem interactive effects. For instance, its codes of good practice cover standard setting, impacts, and assurance,11 with the intention of creating a common understanding among its members on these core and shared aspects of their initia- tives. The policy interactions addressed here are those that emerge from inconsistent practices Trade-offs and interactive effects G. Auld © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 137 Page 13: among similar certiﬁcation programs, which can cause either consumer confusion or mistrust. ISEAL codes, in other words, are similar to the administrative policy developed by a govern- ment’s central agencies that guide the actions of specialized departments and agencies on matters such as standard procedures for contracts, hiring and promotion, or procurement (Pal 2001). Another example of ISEAL’s work is facilitating more speciﬁc and focused coordination projects. The Social Accountability in Sustainable Agriculture project is a prominent example, which involved FLO, Social Accountability International, IFOAM, and the Rainforest Alliance’s program. This project worked on the twofold aim of developing tools and guidelines to improve social audit practices in agricultural systems and driving convergence in the practices of the participating initiatives through mutual learning (Courville 2003). ISEAL has, however, also begun to work more in the area of problem interactions. For instance, the ﬁfth version of its standard setting code has two relevant sections (ISEAL Alliance 2010). First, for standard review and revisions, it guides programs to assess, at least every ﬁve years, whether the standard is still required (Sec 5.11.4).12 Second, it guides programs to ensure they are not duplicating the work of another existing standard (Sec 6.6). Both of these provisions illustrate an attempt by ISEAL to begin to address both policy and problem interactive effects as they guide programs to consider what other existing programs are already covering or what problem gaps persist that their standards might usefully address. The guidelines on review and revision imply that some programs might recognize – through the revision process – that they are no longer necessary, or that, rather than expand their policy focus, they might usefully contract it to better complement other programs. 3.2.5. Supra-sectoral governance The ﬁfth and ﬁnal strategy involves the creation of a supra-sectoral governance institution, which develops new criteria and draws on existing programs to address certain environmental or social performance issues.13 Unlike the last strategy, here the main concern is addressing problem interactive effects. For ISEAL to become a supra-sectoral mechanism, therefore, it would need to create standards about joint certiﬁcation processes equivalent to the bilateral relations discussed under the third strategy. For instance, it could formalize relations between the FSC and some of the agricultural programs to address the extra-sectoral pressures for forest conversion associated with palm oil and soy production. A supra-sectoral mechanism would help account for the inter-issue causes that affect par- ticular policy problems. Such an approach has been proposed by Cashore (2008), who suggests that leading certiﬁcation initiatives could all come under a common “Better World” label that would work to expand the recognition and market uptake of the programs. However, to exem- plify this strategy, the proposal would need to be modiﬁed to include mechanisms that address policy and problem interactive effects. 4. Toward improved issue-boundary management The challenges certiﬁcation programs face in deliberating the trade-offs between specialization and comprehensiveness are not new. Scholars of public administration and international rela- tions have long examined this dilemma in relation to coordination within governments (Christensen & Lægreid 2007) and among the work of international organizations, programs, and convention secretariats (Young 2002; Gehring & Oberthür 2009; Oberthür 2009; Oberthür & Stokke 2011). Autonomous interplay management for private governance First, the strategies identiﬁed above mirror four levels of international regime interplay man- agement deﬁned by Oberthür (2009). These are, ﬁrst, at the highest level, “overarching institu- tional frameworks [require] decision-making beyond the interacting institutions (p. 375).” Second, “joint interplay management (p. 376)” involves institutions coordinating activities and developing joint rules to govern their interactions. Third,“unilateral management by individual institutions (p. 376)” captures independent activities performed by the interacting institutions that occur without any speciﬁc coordination. Finally, the fourth level involves governments, civil society organizations, businesses, and other actors engaging in “autonomous management efforts (p. 376).” For each level, Oberthür (2009) identiﬁes two distinct modes of interplay. Regulatory interplay management entails deﬁning rules to direct behavior, establishing regulatory authority, and, sometimes, implementing and enforcing rules to mitigate conﬂict. Enabling interplay management, by contrast, focuses on learning and capacity; it uses “communication, informa- tion and knowledge” and “the allocation of resources” to gain the support of actors, build capacity, and address knowledge barriers (p. 377).
